1: Standard oracle formula: vayhiy devar-YHWH elai lemor — 'And the word of YHWH came to me saying.' The devar-YHWH (davar, H1697) formula occurs over 50 times in Ezekiel, more than any other book. It is both authorization and content — the word that comes is the word that creates reality.
2: bosér (H1155, 'sour/unripe grapes'): rare word appearing only 4× in the OT (Isaiah 18:5; Jeremiah 31:29-30; Ezekiel 18:2). The proverb — fathers eat unripe grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge — asserts inherited guilt and transferred punishment. YHWH challenges this proverb directly, not because corporate identity is false but because it was being used to deny personal accountability. The same proverb appears in Jeremiah 31:29-30 in the new-covenant context.
3: chay ani (ḥay ʾānî, 'as I live') — the divine oath formula. YHWH swears by his own life since there is no greater: the cessation of the proverb is guaranteed by divine self-commitment. This formula (unique to Ezekiel and Numbers) carries the force of the most solemn oath: YHWH's existence is pledged to the truth of what follows.
4: kol-hanephashoth li hen ('all souls/persons belong to me — both'): nephesh (H5315, dispute_level 3) here means 'person' rather than the interior soul. The dispute in translation centers on whether nephesh is the animating breath-life, the whole person, or the appetite/desire. In this legal-accountability context, it is the person as a moral agent. hanephesh hachot'et hi tamut ('the soul/person who sins, that one shall die'): the stark individual liability principle. tamut from mut (H4191, die) — covenant death, not merely biological.
5: tsaddiq (H6662, 'righteous/just one'): the righteous man is defined by what follows — a list of social, sexual, and cultic behaviors. Tsaddiq in Hebrew is primarily relational-covenantal: one who acts in accordance with the obligations of the covenant relationship (with YHWH and neighbor), not merely one with good inner character. The list that follows is a torah-like checklist of covenant fidelity.
6: lo achal al-heharim ('he has not eaten at the mountain shrines'): cultic separation from idolatry. lo nissa einav el-gillulei beit yisrael ('he has not lifted his eyes to the idols of the house of Israel'): gillulim (H1544, Ezekiel's characteristic word for idols) is a derogatory term related to galal (dung/dung pellets) — a deliberately contemptuous designation. Ezekiel uses gillulim 38 times (more than all other OT books combined). lo timme ('he has not defiled') — tame (H2930, be unclean/defiled): ritual-moral contamination vocabulary from Leviticus applied to sexual ethics.
7: chavlato ('his pledge/garment given as pledge'): chabol (H2254) means both 'to pledge' and 'to bind/destroy.' The returning of a pledge (especially a cloak taken as security for a debt, which Exodus 22:25-26 and Deuteronomy 24:12-13 forbid keeping overnight) is the concretely poor person's protection. lachem nathen ('given food to the hungry') and erOM yechaseh ('covered the naked'): covenant welfare obligations derived from Deut 15 and Lev 19 — these are not works-of-supererogation but required covenant acts.
8: beneshech lo yiten ('he has not lent at interest/usury'): neshek (H5392, 'bite/usury') — the word's root is 'to bite,' vividly depicting interest as the creditor's bite into the debtor. Lending at interest to fellow Israelites was prohibited (Exodus 22:25; Leviticus 25:36-37; Deuteronomy 23:19-20). mishpat emet asah ('he has rendered honest/true judgment'): emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) — judgment that corresponds to reality rather than being distorted by bribery or favoritism (Leviticus 19:15).
9: behuqqotai halach ('he has walked in my statutes'): halak (H1980, walk) as a covenantal metaphor for ongoing moral conduct — a Deuteronomic pattern (Deuteronomy 8:6; Ezekiel 10:12; Ezekiel 11:22). chayyoh yichyeh ('he shall surely live'): the infinitive absolute construction (chayoh yichyeh) emphasizes absolute certainty — this person will certainly, without qualification, live. Chayah (H2421, live) in this covenant context means covenantal life in YHWH's presence, not merely physical survival. Cf. Leviticus 18:5 ('the one who does them will live by them').
10: ben paritz ('a son who is violent/a bandit'): paritz (H6530, violent one, robber) — the son's character is established by a single descriptor before the list of offenses. The contrast with the righteous father creates the generational counter-case: righteousness does not automatically transmit. shofech dam ('who sheds blood'): shaphak dam (H8210 + H1818) — the most serious covenant violation; blood is YHWH's domain (Genesis 9:6; Deuteronomy 19:10).
11: The wicked son reverses each covenant obligation the righteous father kept. The structure is antiphonal: father 'does not eat at the mountain shrines' — son 'eats at the mountain shrines'; father 'does not defile his neighbor's wife' — son 'defiles.' The moral catalogue of Ezekiel 18 is among the most systematic presentations of covenant ethics in the prophets, approaching the form of a legal code (cf. the Decalogue and Lev 19).
12: ani ve-evyon ('poor and needy'): the hendiadys pairing of ani (H6041, afflicted/humble/poor) and evyon (H34, needy/destitute) appears across the Psalms and prophets as the paradigmatic vulnerable person whom the covenant protects (Psalms 72:12-13; Isaiah 41:17; Amos 8:4). Oppressing this pair defines the wicked man as the covenant's antithesis.
13: beneshech natan vetarbit laqach ('he has lent at interest and taken profit'): both neshek (H5392, interest/usury, lit. 'bite') and tarbit (H8636, increase/profit on a loan) appear together here — the legal doublet covers all forms of financial exploitation of the poor. The verdict: mot yumat — damo bo yihyeh ('he shall surely die — his blood is upon himself'). The death is judicial: YHWH holds the debt of blood for covenant violations.
14: The third-generation case: a son who observes his father's wickedness, vayar ('and he sees/considers') — the seeing is morally active, not passive observation. velo yaaseh kahen ('and does not do likewise'): the son's non-conformation to the father's pattern is itself a moral achievement, enabled by the seeing and considering. This counters fatalism about moral inheritance.
15: The third-generation righteous son's list mirrors the first-generation righteous father's list (Ezekiel 18:6-9) nearly verbatim — demonstrating that righteousness is a pattern that can recur across generations even when the intervening generation was wicked. Moral character is neither automatically inherited nor automatically blocked by parental failure.
16: lo hitziq ('he has not oppressed'): hitziq from yatzaq (H3238, oppress/wrong) or more likely atzaq — the not-taking of the pledge, not-robbing, giving food and clothing: the second-generation wicked son's exact opposites, the third generation now mirrors the first in covenant fidelity.
17: lo yamut beavon aviv ('he shall not die for the iniquity of his father'): avon (H5771, iniquity/guilt/punishment for iniquity) — the word covers both the act and its consequence. The son is not absolved of his father's avon on a technicality; rather, YHWH's justice is structured so that individual moral agency is the determining factor, not genealogical inheritance.
18: asher oshaq oshek ('because he practiced cruel oppression'): ashaq (H6231, oppress) doubled for emphasis — persistent, habitual oppression. gozal gazal ('robbed by violence') — gazal (H1497, rob/seize) similarly doubled. The father's death is entirely his own doing: beavono yamut ('in his iniquity he will die'). The covenant's moral economy is precise.
19: The people's objection (lama, 'why?') reflects cognitive dissonance with Deuteronomic theology: the tradition of Exodus 20:5 (visiting iniquity on third and fourth generations) was understood by the exiles as explaining their suffering — but they were using it to escape personal accountability. YHWH's answer reframes the tradition: generational consequences operate at the level of community covenant identity, not individual forensic standing.
20: hanephesh hachot'et hi tamut ('the soul/person that sins — that one shall die'): the principle stated again for emphasis. tzidqat hatzaddiq alav tihyeh verishah harasha alav tihyeh ('the righteousness of the righteous belongs to him, and the wickedness of the wicked falls on him'): tzedaqah (H6666, righteousness/right-doing/right-standing) is not transferable; risha (H7564, wickedness/wrong-doing) is not inflicted on others. This is the bedrock principle of Ezekiel's moral theology.
21: vehu yashuv mikol chatotav ('and he turns from all his sins'): shuv (H7725, turn/return/repent) — the most important repentance verb in the OT. Appears 1,101× in the OT; Ezekiel uses it intensively. The turn is from (min) sins toward (el) YHWH's statutes. chayoh yichyeh lo yamut ('he shall surely live, he shall not die'): the double statement (positive then negative) is the strongest possible legal guarantee of life. The turning itself opens the forensic file of life.
22: kol-peshaav ('all his transgressions'): pesha (H6588, rebellion/transgression) — the most serious Hebrew sin term, denoting willful breach of a covenant relationship (as between vassal and suzerain). lo yizakeru lo ('will not be remembered against him'): zakar (H2142, remember) in a covenant context: YHWH's 'not remembering' is a covenantal act of amnesty, not mere forgetfulness. The forgiven pesha is sealed shut in the divine ledger — a promise with legal force.
23: hachapots achapots beMOT harasha ('do I really delight in the death of the wicked?'): the rhetorical question is divine self-revelation. chaphets (H2654, delight/desire/take pleasure in) — YHWH takes no pleasure in covenant death sentences; justice is not his goal, life is. im-lo veshuvo miderachav vechai ('rather that he should turn from his ways and live'): the divine will expressed in the im-lo ('is it not rather') construction. This verse is the theological heart of the chapter.
24: uveshuv hatzaddiq mitzidqato ('when the righteous person turns from their righteousness'): the counter-case: the righteous person who turns away forfeits their accumulated righteousness. kol-tzidqotav asher asah lo yizakeru ('none of their righteous deeds will be remembered'): the negative counterpart of Ezekiel 18:22 — just as the forgiven sinner's record is cleared, the apostate's righteous record is wiped. The ledger is always current, not cumulative-permanent in either direction.
25: lo yitaken derech YHWH ('the way of the LORD is not fair/right'): the people's accusation against YHWH's justice. takan (H8505, to weigh out/measure/make right/be equitable) — used of accurate weights (Proverbs 16:2; Ezekiel 21:2; Ezekiel 24:12). The accusation is that YHWH's moral accounting is not properly balanced. YHWH reverses the charge: it is their ways that are not takan.
26: The principle restated: shuv from righteousness toward iniquity → death; the iniquity he commited (beavono asher asah) is the cause. YHWH's statement is not harsh but logical: if one chooses iniquity over the covenant relationship, one chooses the consequence.
27: uveshuv rasha merishtato ('when the wicked person turns from their wickedness'): risha (H7564, wickedness) → shuv (turn) → doing what is just and right (mishpat utzedaqah). hu yechayeh et-nafsho ('he will save his own life/soul'): literally 'he will keep his nephesh alive.' The turning person is the agent of their own life-keeping — YHWH's justice rewards the genuine turning.
28: vayyar vayashav ('because he has considered and turned'): the sequence of seeing-and-turning is the moral process Ezekiel envisions. ra'ah (H7200, see) here means moral reflection — taking stock of one's trajectory. The turning follows from genuine moral sight, which is why the wicked son of Ezekiel 18:14 who 'sees and does not do likewise' is the positive counterpart.
29: The accusation repeated a second time: lo yitaken derech YHWH. YHWH's answer is consistent: he will judge each one by their own ways (ish kidrachav). The repetition (also in Ezekiel 18:25) signals the depth of the dispute — this is not academic theology but the exiles' actual complaint against the justice of their situation.
30: shuvu vehashivu mikol-pisheichem ('repent and turn from all your transgressions'): the double imperative — shuv twice (repent! and turn!) — is the pastoral application of the theology. velo-yihyeh lachem lemichshol avon ('so that iniquity will not be your downfall'): michshol (H4383, stumbling-block/occasion of falling) — the accumulated iniquity that causes one to stumble. Repentance preempts the stumbling.
31: hashlichu mealeichem et-kol-pisheeichem ('cast away from you all your transgressions'): active imperative — the human agent must throw off the weight of accumulated rebellion. vaasu lachem lev chadash veruach chadasha ('make for yourselves a new heart and a new spirit'): the command that anticipates its own impossibility, resolved in Ezekiel 36:26-27 where YHWH promises to give what he here commands. The gap between command and ability is the pneumatological space that the new covenant fills.
32: ki lo achapots beMOT haMet ('for I have no pleasure in the death of anyone who dies'): haMet — 'the one who dies,' emphasizing that this is about actual deaths, not abstract principles. shuvu vechyu ('turn and live!'): the two-word summary of the entire chapter. Ezekiel's prophetic imperative at its briefest and most urgent. The entire chapter is a theological framework for the possibility and urgency of this simple command.
